meta.setting: Brush-choked fire line above Clear Creek
meta.rdr_clone_target: PS3-era RDR-clone western art, grounded in labor, smoke, wildlife, and rugged travel

research_grounding.title: NPS The Wintu
research_grounding.use: Fire stewardship context around Clear Creek and Wintu homeland.

research_grounding.title: California Department of Fish and Wildlife Black Bear
research_grounding.use: Black bear habitat and sign in brush, forest, and camp edges.

image_prompt.subject: a mixed brush crew cutting a fire line through chaparral and pine slash, with smoke low on the ridge and a black bear track pressed into soft dirt near the foreground
image_prompt.scene: hot afternoon; axes, crosscut saw, canteens, sweat-dark shirts, brush piles, orange light through smoke, one worker pausing to read the track before the crew advances
image_prompt.composition: square work scene with a diagonal fire line dividing safer ground from brush-choked risk; tools and bear sign anchor the foreground, smoke and crew rhythm fill the middle
image_prompt.camera: shoulder-height side angle with tools in motion, readable animal sign, and landscape pressure visible behind
image_prompt.hand_cut_note: Make the crew look skilled and tired, not heroic. Fire-line work is measured in blisters, and the bear track should feel like the hill adding its own opinion.

style_modifiers.medium: PS3-era open-world western game art, early-HD in-engine labor and landscape scene
render_traits: slightly simplified worker silhouettes and tool geometry
modest texture resolution on brush, ash, cloth, dirt, and metal
hard ambient occlusion under brush piles, boots, saws, and stones
smoky orange daylight with gritty visibility
believable 1899 hand tools and work clothing
style_modifiers.palette: smoke orange, ash gray, brush green, dirt umber, sweat-dark blue, iron gray, canteen tin, and pine shadow.

text_rendering.instructions: No readable signs, labels, crew marks, or text.

negative_prompt: copy of commercial game characters, missions, towns, or logos
cropped tools or cropped bear track

self_check: Reads as PS3-era western game art at square page-card scale.
The brush crew, tools, fire line, smoke, and bear sign are all fully readable.
Every visible prop and garment belongs plausibly to 1899 brush work.
Fire and wildlife are grounded pressure, not spectacle.
No copied game characters, readable modern text, or inherited non-western symbols.
